title: Dhamma: Sacca (Truthfulness)
description: Sacca — truth — is one of the Ten Perfections. The Buddha lived by truth, spoke truth, and taught the truth of suffering, its cause, its cessation, and the path to liberation.

title: Greensboro Sit-Ins Begin
description: On February 1, 1960, four African-American college students — Ezell Blair Jr., David Richmond, Franklin McCain, and Joseph McNeil — sat down at a whites-only Woolworth's lunch counter in Greensboro, North Carolina, and refused to leave. Their courageous act of nonviolent protest sparked a wave of sit-ins across the American South and became a pivotal moment in the Civil Rights Movement.

title: First Session of the US Supreme Court
description: On February 1, 1790, the Supreme Court of the United States convened for the first time at the Royal Exchange building in New York City, with Chief Justice John Jay presiding. The establishment of the nation's highest court was a cornerstone of American constitutional governance and the separation of powers.

title: World Wetlands Day
description: A day to raise awareness about the vital role of wetlands for people and the planet. Caring for the environment is an expression of Dhamma — respect for all life.

title: Battle of Stalingrad Ends
description: On February 2, 1943, the remnants of the German 6th Army under Field Marshal Friedrich Paulus surrendered to Soviet forces at Stalingrad. This decisive Soviet victory marked a major turning point in World War II, shattering the myth of Nazi invincibility and shifting the momentum of the war toward the Allied powers.

title: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo Signed
description: On February 2, 1848, the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo was signed, ending the Mexican-American War. Mexico ceded a vast territory including present-day California, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, and parts of several other states to the United States, reshaping the map of North America.

title: Dhamma: The Four Noble Truths
description: The foundation of Buddha's teaching: Dukkha (suffering exists), Samudaya (it has a cause), Nirodha (it can end), Magga (the Eightfold Path leads to its end).

title: 15th Amendment Ratified
description: On February 3, 1870, the 15th Amendment to the United States Constitution was ratified, declaring that the right to vote shall not be denied on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude. It was a landmark victory for formerly enslaved people and the abolitionist movement, though its promise would be undermined by decades of Jim Crow laws.

title: Luna 9 Lands on the Moon
description: On February 3, 1966, the Soviet spacecraft Luna 9 became the first human-made object to achieve a soft landing on the Moon and transmit photographs back to Earth. This historic achievement proved that the lunar surface could support a landing craft and paved the way for future crewed missions.

title: World Cancer Day
description: A global initiative to raise awareness about cancer prevention and treatment. Compassion for the sick and suffering is a core Buddhist value.

title: Rosa Parks Born
description: Rosa Louise McCauley Parks was born on February 4, 1913, in Tuskegee, Alabama. Known as the 'Mother of the Civil Rights Movement,' her refusal to give up her bus seat to a white passenger in Montgomery in 1955 ignited the Montgomery Bus Boycott and became a defining symbol of resistance against racial segregation.

title: Facebook Founded
description: On February 4, 2004, Mark Zuckerberg launched 'TheFacebook' from his Harvard University dorm room. What began as a college social network rapidly grew into the world's largest social media platform, fundamentally transforming how billions of people communicate, share information, and organize movements.

title: Kashmir Day / Dhamma Reflection
description: A day to reflect on peace and human rights. The Buddha taught: 'Hatred does not cease by hatred, but only by love; this is the eternal rule.'

title: Mexican Constitution Adopted
description: On February 5, 1917, Mexico adopted its revolutionary new constitution following years of armed uprising against the Porfirio Díaz dictatorship. The Constitution of 1917 was one of the most progressive in the world at the time, enshrining labor rights, land reform, and the separation of church and state.

title: Apollo 14 Lands on the Moon
description: On February 5, 1971, Apollo 14's lunar module Antares, carrying astronauts Alan Shepard and Edgar Mitchell, landed on the Moon's Fra Mauro highlands. Shepard became the fifth person to walk on the Moon and famously hit two golf balls on the lunar surface, while the mission restored confidence in NASA after the near-disaster of Apollo 13.

title: Mahaparinirvana Diwas
description: Remembrance of the Mahaparinirvana of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar on 6 December 1956. This day is observed to reflect on his extraordinary contributions to social justice, constitutional rights, and the revival of Buddhism in India.

title: Franco-American Treaty of Alliance
description: On February 6, 1778, France and the United States signed the Treaty of Alliance and the Treaty of Amity and Commerce in Paris, formally recognizing American independence and establishing a military alliance. French support proved decisive in the American Revolution, culminating in the British surrender at Yorktown in 1781.

title: SpaceX Falcon Heavy First Launch
description: On February 6, 2018, SpaceX successfully launched the Falcon Heavy, the most powerful operational rocket in the world at the time, from Kennedy Space Center. The mission included the spectacular synchronized landing of two side boosters and sent Elon Musk's Tesla Roadster into a heliocentric orbit as a demonstration payload.

title: Dhamma: Right Action (Sammā Kammanta)
description: Right Action means abstaining from killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct. It is ethical conduct rooted in compassion and respect for all living beings.

title: Charles Dickens Born
description: Charles John Huffam Dickens was born on February 7, 1812, in Portsmouth, England. His novels — including Oliver Twist, A Tale of Two Cities, and David Copperfield — exposed the brutal realities of poverty, child labor, and social inequality in Victorian England, arousing public conscience and inspiring reforms.

title: Grenada Gains Independence
description: On February 7, 1974, Grenada gained independence from British colonial rule after nearly two centuries of control. Led by Prime Minister Eric Gairy, the Caribbean island nation became a sovereign state, joining the wave of decolonization that swept through the region in the post-war era.

title: Dhamma: Adhiṭṭhāna (Determination)
description: Adhiṭṭhāna is the perfection of firm resolve. The Bodhisatta's unwavering determination to attain enlightenment for the benefit of all beings is a model for all seekers.

title: Mary Queen of Scots Executed
description: On February 8, 1587, Mary Stuart, Queen of Scots, was executed at Fotheringhay Castle in England after being convicted of plotting against Queen Elizabeth I. Her execution was a dramatic climax to decades of religious and political conflict between Catholic and Protestant factions in Britain and Europe.

title: Jules Verne Born
description: Jules Gabriel Verne was born on February 8, 1828, in Nantes, France. Often called the 'Father of Science Fiction,' his visionary novels — Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea, Around the World in Eighty Days, and From the Earth to the Moon — anticipated submarines, space travel, and air travel decades before they became reality.

title: Dhamma: Sangha — The Community
description: The Sangha is the community of noble disciples who walk the path together. 'Admirable friendship is the whole of the holy life,' the Buddha told Ananda.

title: Boeing 747 Maiden Flight
description: On February 9, 1969, the Boeing 747, the world's first wide-body commercial airliner, made its maiden flight from Paine Field in Everett, Washington. Known as the 'Jumbo Jet,' the 747 revolutionized international air travel by dramatically increasing passenger capacity and making long-distance flying accessible to millions.

title: Battle of Guadalcanal Ends
description: On February 9, 1943, the United States declared Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands secured after six months of brutal fighting against Japanese forces. The campaign was the first major Allied offensive in the Pacific Theater of World War II, halting Japanese expansion and marking a strategic turning point in the war.

title: World Pulses Day
description: A UN observance promoting nutritious and sustainable food. Vegetarian and plant-based diets align with the Buddhist precept of non-harm (ahiṃsā) to all living beings.

title: Treaty of Paris (1763) Signed
description: On February 10, 1763, the Treaty of Paris was signed, ending the Seven Years' War (known as the French and Indian War in North America). France ceded Canada and its territories east of the Mississippi to Britain, fundamentally reshaping the colonial map of the Americas and setting the stage for the American Revolution.

title: Deep Blue Defeats Kasparov
description: On February 10, 1996, IBM's Deep Blue chess computer defeated reigning world champion Garry Kasparov in the first game of their historic match, marking the first time a computer beat a world chess champion under standard tournament conditions. This landmark event signaled a new era in artificial intelligence and human-machine competition.

title: Magha Puja
description: An important Buddhist festival commemorating the spontaneous gathering of 1,250 enlightened arahants before the Buddha at Veluvana, where he delivered the Ovadapatimokkha — the core principles of Buddhism.

title: Nelson Mandela Released from Prison
description: On February 11, 1990, Nelson Mandela walked free from Victor Verster Prison near Cape Town after 27 years of imprisonment by the apartheid regime of South Africa. His release was a watershed moment in the global anti-apartheid struggle and paved the way for the dismantling of institutionalized racial segregation and the birth of a democratic South Africa.

title: Iranian Revolution Triumphs
description: On February 11, 1979, the Iranian monarchy collapsed as revolutionary forces loyal to Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini overwhelmed troops loyal to Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi. The revolution ended 2,500 years of Persian monarchy and established the Islamic Republic of Iran, profoundly reshaping the politics of the Middle East.

title: Abraham Lincoln Jayanti
description: Birth anniversary of Abraham Lincoln, the 16th US President who abolished slavery. His fight for human equality resonates with the values of Dhamma and social justice worldwide.

title: Sant Ravidas Jayanti
description: Birth anniversary of Sant Ravidas (c. 1377–1527), the great Bhakti saint, poet, and social reformer who rejected caste hierarchy and preached the equality of all human beings. Revered across the Bahujan community, his verses in the Guru Granth Sahib proclaim: 'Begumpura — the city without sorrow — where there is no suffering, no tax, no hierarchy.'

title: NAACP Founded
description: On February 12, 1909, the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) was founded in New York City by a coalition of activists including W.E.B. Du Bois, Ida B. Wells, and Mary White Ovington. Chosen deliberately on Abraham Lincoln's centennial birthday, the NAACP became the most influential civil rights organization in American history, fighting racial injustice through legal advocacy and grassroots activism.

title: Sarojini Naidu Jayanti
description: Birth anniversary of Sarojini Naidu, the 'Nightingale of India,' a poet, freedom fighter, and the first Indian woman to become a state governor.

title: Glorious Revolution: William and Mary Proclaimed
description: On February 13, 1689, William of Orange and Mary Stuart were proclaimed joint sovereigns of England following the Glorious Revolution, which deposed the Catholic King James II. This bloodless revolution established the supremacy of Parliament over the Crown and led to the English Bill of Rights, a foundational document for modern constitutional democracy.

title: Galileo Arrives in Rome for Inquisition Trial
description: On February 13, 1633, Galileo Galilei arrived in Rome to stand trial before the Roman Inquisition for advocating the heliocentric model of the solar system. His trial became one of history's most famous confrontations between scientific inquiry and religious authority, ultimately resulting in his forced recantation and house arrest for the rest of his life.

title: Maitri Diwas
description: A day to celebrate universal love, compassion, and Maitri (loving-kindness) towards all beings — the core teaching of Buddha Dhamma. Spread goodwill and practice compassion.

title: Alexander Graham Bell Files Telephone Patent
description: On February 14, 1876, Alexander Graham Bell filed his patent application for the telephone at the US Patent Office, just hours before rival inventor Elisha Gray filed a similar caveat. Bell was granted Patent No. 174,465 on March 7, 1876, for what would become one of the most transformative inventions in human history, revolutionizing global communication.

title: Frederick Douglass Birthday
description: Frederick Douglass, born into slavery in Maryland around February 1818, chose February 14 as his birthday. He escaped bondage to become the most prominent African-American abolitionist, orator, and writer of the 19th century. His autobiographies and fiery speeches against slavery made him a towering figure in the fight for human freedom and racial equality.

title: Dhamma: Paññā (Wisdom)
description: Paññā is the perfection of wisdom — seeing things as they truly are. Wisdom arises through hearing the Dhamma, reflecting on it, and meditating on the nature of reality.

title: Galileo Galilei Born
description: Galileo Galilei was born on February 15, 1564, in Pisa, Italy. Called the 'Father of Modern Science,' he made groundbreaking contributions to astronomy, physics, and the scientific method. His telescopic observations — including the moons of Jupiter and the phases of Venus — provided crucial evidence for the Copernican heliocentric model of the solar system.

title: USS Maine Explodes in Havana Harbor
description: On February 15, 1898, the United States battleship USS Maine exploded and sank in Havana Harbor, Cuba, killing 266 crew members. The incident, blamed on Spain though its true cause remains debated, inflamed American public opinion and became the rallying cry 'Remember the Maine!' that propelled the United States into the Spanish-American War.

title: Dhamma: Right Livelihood (Sammā Ājīva)
description: Right Livelihood means earning one's living in a way that does not cause harm. The Buddha prohibited trade in weapons, living beings, meat, intoxicants, and poisons.

title: Howard Carter Opens Tutankhamun's Burial Chamber
description: On February 16, 1923, British archaeologist Howard Carter opened the sealed burial chamber of Pharaoh Tutankhamun in the Valley of the Kings, Egypt, revealing the spectacular golden sarcophagus and treasures within. The discovery of the nearly intact 3,000-year-old tomb became one of the most famous archaeological finds in history and captivated the world.

title: First 911 Emergency Call Made
description: On February 16, 1968, the first 911 emergency telephone call in the United States was made in Haleyville, Alabama, by Alabama Speaker of the House Rankin Fite. The creation of the universal emergency number revolutionized public safety and emergency response, eventually becoming the standard across North America.

title: Dhamma: The Middle Way
description: The Buddha discovered the Middle Way — avoiding both extreme indulgence and extreme austerity. This balanced approach is the foundation of the Noble Eightfold Path.

title: Giordano Bruno Burned at the Stake
description: On February 17, 1600, Italian philosopher, mathematician, and cosmological theorist Giordano Bruno was burned at the stake in Rome's Campo de' Fiori by the Roman Inquisition for heresy. Bruno championed the idea of an infinite universe with many worlds, anticipating modern cosmology. His martyrdom became a symbol of the struggle for free thought against religious dogma.

title: Thomas Jefferson Elected President by House
description: On February 17, 1801, the US House of Representatives chose Thomas Jefferson as the third President of the United States after 36 ballots to break an Electoral College tie with Aaron Burr. This peaceful resolution of a constitutional crisis demonstrated the resilience of American democracy and led to the 12th Amendment reforming presidential elections.

title: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa Jayanti
description: Birth anniversary of Sri Ramakrishna, the mystic whose life inspired Swami Vivekananda and the Ramakrishna Mission. He taught the harmony of all religions and the divinity within.

title: Discovery of Pluto
description: On February 18, 1930, American astronomer Clyde Tombaugh discovered Pluto at the Lowell Observatory in Flagstaff, Arizona, by comparing photographic plates taken in January. For over 75 years it was classified as the ninth planet of the solar system, and its discovery expanded our understanding of the outer reaches of the solar system.

title: White Rose Resistance Members Arrested
description: On February 18, 1943, Hans and Sophie Scholl, student members of the White Rose anti-Nazi resistance group, were arrested by the Gestapo at the University of Munich after distributing leaflets calling for an end to the war and the Nazi regime. They were tried and executed just four days later, becoming enduring symbols of courage and moral resistance against tyranny.

title: Chhatrapati Shivaji Jayanti
description: Birth anniversary of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj, the legendary Maratha warrior king who championed self-rule (Swarajya), justice, religious tolerance, and the welfare of common people.

title: Nicolaus Copernicus Born
description: Nicolaus Copernicus was born on February 19, 1473, in Toruń, Royal Prussia. His revolutionary work De revolutionibus orbium coelestium (1543) proposed the heliocentric model, placing the Sun rather than the Earth at the center of the universe. This 'Copernican Revolution' fundamentally transformed astronomy and the scientific worldview.

title: Executive Order 9066: Japanese American Internment
description: On February 19, 1942, President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 9066, authorizing the forced relocation and internment of approximately 120,000 Japanese Americans from the West Coast into concentration camps. This act of racial injustice, driven by wartime hysteria and prejudice, remains one of the gravest violations of civil liberties in American history.

title: World Day of Social Justice
description: A UN observance promoting social justice, including efforts to address poverty, exclusion, and inequality. Dr. Ambedkar devoted his life to this very cause.

title: John Glenn Orbits the Earth
description: On February 20, 1962, astronaut John Glenn aboard Friendship 7 became the first American to orbit the Earth, completing three orbits during a nearly five-hour mission. His historic flight boosted American morale during the Space Race with the Soviet Union and made Glenn a national hero and symbol of the American space program.

title: Lord Mountbatten Appointed Last Viceroy of India
description: On February 20, 1947, British Prime Minister Clement Attlee announced that Lord Louis Mountbatten would serve as the last Viceroy of India, tasked with overseeing the transfer of power from British colonial rule. This appointment set in motion the final chapter of Indian independence, culminating in the birth of the sovereign nations of India and Pakistan on August 15, 1947.

title: International Mother Language Day
description: UNESCO day promoting linguistic and cultural diversity. Language is the carrier of culture, identity, and the oral traditions through which Dhamma has been transmitted for millennia.

title: Malcolm X Assassinated
description: On February 21, 1965, Malcolm X, the influential African-American leader and human rights activist, was assassinated while speaking at the Audubon Ballroom in New York City. Born Malcolm Little, he transformed from a convicted criminal into one of the most articulate and powerful advocates for Black empowerment, self-defense, and pan-African unity in the 20th century.

title: Communist Manifesto Published
description: On February 21, 1848, Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels published The Communist Manifesto in London. This revolutionary pamphlet, with its famous opening line about a 'spectre haunting Europe,' analyzed class struggle, criticized capitalism, and called for the working class to unite. It became one of the most influential political documents in history, shaping revolutionary movements worldwide.

title: Dhamma: Anattā (Non-Self)
description: Anattā is the Buddhist teaching that there is no permanent, unchanging self. Understanding this liberates the mind from ego-driven suffering and leads to true freedom.

title: George Washington Born
description: George Washington was born on February 22, 1732, in Westmoreland County, Virginia. As commander-in-chief of the Continental Army during the American Revolution and the first President of the United States, he played an indispensable role in the founding of the American republic. His voluntary relinquishment of power set a precedent for democratic governance.

title: Cloning of Dolly the Sheep Announced
description: On February 22, 1997, scientists at the Roslin Institute in Edinburgh, Scotland, announced the successful cloning of Dolly the sheep — the first mammal cloned from an adult somatic cell. Created by Ian Wilmut and Keith Campbell, Dolly's birth revolutionized biology and ignited worldwide debates about the ethics, possibilities, and limits of genetic science.

title: Dhamma: Dependent Origination
description: Paṭiccasamuppāda — Dependent Origination — reveals how suffering arises through a chain of conditions. Understanding this chain is the key to breaking free from the cycle of rebirth.

title: Sant Gadge Baba Jayanti
description: Birth anniversary of Sant Gadge Baba (Debuji Zhingraji Janorkar, 1876–1956), the wandering saint of Maharashtra who devoted his life to cleanliness, education, and the upliftment of the downtrodden. He rejected caste superstition and idol worship, preaching social reform through selfless service, hygiene, and rational thought.

title: W.E.B. Du Bois Born
description: William Edward Burghardt Du Bois was born on February 23, 1868, in Great Barrington, Massachusetts. A pioneering sociologist, historian, and civil rights activist, he was the first African American to earn a doctorate from Harvard and co-founded the NAACP. His seminal work The Souls of Black Folk and his concept of 'double consciousness' profoundly shaped the struggle for racial justice.

title: Central Excise Day
description: Honors India's Central Excise department, established in 1944. Good governance and fair taxation are pillars of a just society envisioned by the Constitution.

title: Marbury v. Madison Decided
description: On February 24, 1803, the US Supreme Court under Chief Justice John Marshall issued its landmark ruling in Marbury v. Madison, establishing the principle of judicial review — the power of the courts to declare laws unconstitutional. This decision fundamentally shaped the balance of power in American government and became a cornerstone of constitutional law worldwide.

title: Impeachment of President Andrew Johnson
description: On February 24, 1868, the US House of Representatives voted to impeach President Andrew Johnson, making him the first sitting president to be impeached. Johnson was charged with violating the Tenure of Office Act by attempting to remove Secretary of War Edwin Stanton. He was later acquitted by the Senate by a single vote.

title: Dhamma: Viriya (Energy/Effort)
description: Viriya is the perfection of energy and effort. The Buddha taught that enlightenment requires persistent, balanced effort — neither too tense nor too lax, like a well-tuned string.

title: Hiram Revels: First Black US Senator
description: On February 25, 1870, Hiram Rhodes Revels of Mississippi was sworn in as the first African-American member of the United States Senate, filling the seat once held by Confederate president Jefferson Davis. His election during Reconstruction was a powerful symbol of the transformative promise of emancipation, though the gains of this era would soon be rolled back by Jim Crow.

title: People Power Revolution Triumphs in the Philippines
description: On February 25, 1986, the People Power Revolution (EDSA Revolution) in the Philippines culminated as dictator Ferdinand Marcos fled the country and Corazon Aquino was inaugurated as president. Millions of Filipinos had gathered on EDSA highway in a peaceful mass uprising, making it one of the most successful nonviolent revolutions in modern history.

title: Veer Savarkar Jayanti
description: Birth anniversary of Vinayak Damodar Savarkar, a freedom fighter and political thinker who was imprisoned in the Cellular Jail, Andaman, for his role in the independence movement.

title: Robert Watson-Watt Demonstrates Radar
description: On February 26, 1935, Scottish physicist Robert Watson-Watt conducted the first practical demonstration of radar at Daventry, England, detecting a Handley Page Heyford bomber aircraft at a range of eight miles using reflected radio waves. This breakthrough technology proved critical to Britain's defense in the Battle of Britain and revolutionized aviation, navigation, and meteorology.

title: Grand Canyon National Park Established
description: On February 26, 1919, President Woodrow Wilson signed the act establishing the Grand Canyon as a US National Park, protecting one of the world's most awe-inspiring natural wonders. The canyon, carved by the Colorado River over millions of years, is sacred to several Native American tribes and attracts millions of visitors annually.

title: Chandrashekhar Azad Punyatithi
description: Remembrance of Chandrashekhar Azad, the revolutionary freedom fighter who chose death over surrender at Alfred Park, Allahabad, in the struggle for Indian independence.

title: Dominican Republic Independence Day
description: On February 27, 1844, the Dominican Republic declared its independence from Haiti after 22 years of Haitian rule. Led by Juan Pablo Duarte and the secret society La Trinitaria, the bloodless revolution established the Dominican Republic as a sovereign nation, making it one of the earliest independence movements in the Caribbean.

title: Reichstag Fire
description: On February 27, 1933, the German Reichstag (parliament building) in Berlin was set ablaze in an act of arson. The Nazi regime under Adolf Hitler used the fire as a pretext to declare a state of emergency, suspend civil liberties, and consolidate dictatorial power through the Reichstag Fire Decree. It marked a decisive step in the destruction of democracy in Germany.

title: National Science Day
description: Commemorating C.V. Raman's discovery of the Raman Effect in 1928. Science and rational inquiry align with the Buddha's emphasis on investigation and direct experience over blind faith.

title: Watson and Crick Discover DNA's Double Helix
description: On February 28, 1953, James Watson and Francis Crick at the Cavendish Laboratory in Cambridge completed their model of the double helix structure of DNA, the molecule of heredity. Building on X-ray crystallography data from Rosalind Franklin and Maurice Wilkins, their discovery unlocked the secret of how genetic information is stored and replicated, launching the era of molecular biology.

title: Republican Party Founded
description: On February 28, 1854, a coalition of anti-slavery activists, former Whigs, and Free Soilers gathered in a schoolhouse in Ripon, Wisconsin, to establish the Republican Party. Founded primarily to oppose the expansion of slavery into new US territories, the party rapidly grew in influence and elected Abraham Lincoln as president just six years later.
